title: aloha from hawaii
content: did many felt elvis is outdated in the 70s? And how many people watched aloha from hawaii... Oh it seems so insane though when i think about this. the record sound like someone singing from the 50s (which he is, at least peaked around that time) on the moon (which the album seem to suggest, I still don't know where the heck he is singing lol), broadcasting and delivering the performance in the newest form of technology, and around 500-600 million people watched the bloody performance. I mean it doesn't get more insane than that. The last time I felt this insane is when I find out the Beatles wrote all of their songs and produced their albums in 6-7 years lol. And I can't believe he sang Something on the show you know. It's literally my favourite Beatles song. No deny.

title: i've been betrayed
content:
Listening to Images 1 & 2 by Debussy is some of the most intense things I allow my heart to endure through. I was expecting impressionist artist like Debussy to be quite chill. With mimicking the flickering of light I was expecting to be the ultimate natural and transparent trip that no freaking psychedelics or 2001 will match. It will literally just colourful but natural light flashing through my eyes, not intense, but transparent you know. Boy was I wrong. I don’t know what the fuck about the music theory, but listening to this is like not sleeping during midnight you know. My blood pressure is so goddamn high with the de de de de de de de. Fuck. It’s too damn fast and technical and quiet and loud at the same time like fucking Trump you know. I mean if this’s Debussy best work I would say he failed you know. Heck I even put on Monet artworks for the sake of maximising my enjoyment while listening to Debussy, which btw, I only enjoy his three of his paintings, “Waterloo Bridge, London, at Dusk”, “Waterloo Bridge”, and “Charing Cross Bridge: Fog on the Thames” because they only focus on light and colour. All this other paintings are just objects with impressionist filters; it’s not that abstract or has depth in the feeling you know.  Because with those three paintings list above you dream however the fuck you want. You’re in control of your heartbeat and feeling. It’s a smooth ride.
But you know I don't mind the energy for rock music you know like Led Zeppelin or Paranoid Android or whatever. I dig it. But Debussy is just so sharp you know. He's too damn sharp, like a shattered mirror some might say, or a surgeon.

title: human
content: It takes approximately 10 seconds for someone to die, and 1 bullet that is. 1 second for making the bullet itself, 1 second for pulling the trigger, and the rest of the seconds for you to struggle and reminisce your life. It doesn’t matter how sexy, stupid, intelligent, strong, weak, or fucked up you are. Everything you have gone through up to that point - the people you fucked, the love you made and take, the electricity and water and food you consumed that ultimately caused the death of several lives you may never know etc. It all disappears in that 10 seconds. Your parents will mourn for you, that's for sure. And so are your loved ones. Or those you yearned to love you. So why aren’t people more humble than they’re?

title: beethoven and debussy
content: I finally know what I like from Classical Music, and it’s very specific coming from my background of rock music, as you know. I love love love Beethoven Symphony Nos.6 and by this composer specifically. It’s literally sunshine and moss and tranquil and me eyes are seeing barley and fields. A beautiful scenery or painting created effortlessly, like a painter like Van Gogh would create you know. I’m playing this on my iPhone right now and the phone is literally feels like floating on my hands because how light and transparent and silky the music felt. Secondly, I love Debussy La Mer from this album specifically. Again the atmosphere and imagery is so strong that I can’t escape it, albeit I would say the feeling is not as strong as with Beethoven Pastoral, but I think that’s because I haven’t listen to the whole album yet, so my opinion is doom to be bias and so to early to determine. But yeah here are my views

title: radiohead pipe dream
content:
I hope Radiohead's next album is a rock experimental opera lol
A rock opera for Akira you know. Dystopian vibe with a crazy introspective narrator and protagonist that find his way in the digital age but the music make us sounds like we're living in the void all the time kind of thing you know.
Classical music aren't like rock music, where the mixing is important for creating the atmosphere they want to create in a literal sense. Like you could create whatever the fuck you want down to the sensory and your soul essentially, and no one does it better than Radiohead. But I believe there're still merit in classical music for accomplishing so, just a bit traditional. Are there any popular composers that are like atmospheric thing you know. Radiohead tracks for references: Pyramid Song, No Surprises, Sail to the Moon

title: lovely quote
content:
Scan not a friend with a microscopic glass — You know his faults — Then let his foibles pass
George Harrison’s The Answer’s at the End

title: mindless
content:
My mind is glooming
My mind still hanging on a cliff
And in a tiny little cabinet
Which you and I visit every half-a-week
Although it seems to me that I’m half-asleep
It’s you that’s still stuck on this bridge
With no ends meet
No ends meeting

title: my take on radiohead's genius
content:
Kid A: Thom Yorke described it as "looking at the fire from afar"—more detached, cold, and synthetic.
Amnesiac: Described as "standing in the fire"—more aggressive, human, and "organic".
Kid A is a cold, expansive landscape, Amnesiac feels like being trapped in a dusty, dimly lit room with the band.
I mean the best way to create atmosphere is to be like Radiohead, where their sonic soundscapes and melodic notes actually pay off and the emotional and surreal depth is actually deeper without those stupid sound effects to assist. I mean people say Pink Floyd are creative, but I think quite the opposite. The true musical creatives is to make the music literally vibrate and live inside you like Radiohead, and this is shown in tunes like Sail to the Moon and No Surprises. If Pink Floyd exists with Radiohead in the same period they will be smashed, and the majority will agree with me.

title: first hand experience with hypnagogia
preview: fasten your seatbelt. reading this will be a trip (literally)
content: I was sitting in a car to Guangzhou in the night just now for school from Shenzhen. I was watching Led Zeppelin Stairway to Heaven performance and it was absolutely stunning. I then move on to Radiohead's Berlin concert. I picked a few specific numbers from the concert to listen to (because the concert length was longer than the travel length); I first listened to No Surprises and it was like fairies have went on a glossy quiet visceral journey to find the pipers in the woods and decided to go dancing in my brain. I have obviously heard the song a million times and in a variety of situations, but this concert's interpretation has give me this feeling. My favourite moment of listening No Surprises is also the most peaceful moment, (ironically speaking, since the song is really a quiet human desperation), which is during the midnight 1AM sitting on my bed like a drunk dude. I'm sitting on my bed, and I'm looking at the world of  sparkles warm and crystalline through my huge open glass pane of my room (I live in Shenzhen). It was my ultimate dance - the joined romantic beauty between my  ironically skeptical view of technology as a programmer and my outpouring love for art and music. That rain of thoughts just washes over me as I was watching the concert...Anyway back to the story I’m trying to tell. I was sitting in the car, right? After I listened to No Surprises, I listened to Daydreaming, to continue my subconscious deep dive. I got what I wanted...literally. I was listening to it, and on the halfway through, I node off, though not entirely. It was like my experience with watching Cries and Whispers, where halfway through the film (when Agnes “dies”), I went into a state that I could only surreal retrospective wise (I’m just a high school student; I’m not on drugs lol), where I was in this subconscious dream, thinking about my past and predicting what will happen in my life while simultaneously dreaming and “predicting” or watching the film where my eyes are half closed and I do it only through the sound of the film (which you know is almost adding up to nothing). When I woke up, it’s 3/4 through the film, but I know EXACTLY what’s going on. Though I’m not predicting song structures, I was feeling the song all the way through even though my mind is strained and my eyes are hardly opened. When I “woke up”, I saw tiny dancing lights and light streams flowing and melting past my brain, but really I was just looking the world through the car’s windshield. It was slow but momentous and gradual. It was like waiting for someone to come. It was surreal, and I like this feeling, very very much. I switched off the concert on YouTube (I forgot which song I was listening to from the concert by the time I switched it off because I was so soaked in with the experience), and I immediately went with my goto song for subconscious experience in the night - Nude by Radiohead. The lyrics always spoke to me, because I have that outburst of urge for love that come out of nowhere sometimes, and every time I thought about this, I thought I would go to hell for what my dirty mind is thinking (identical to the lyrics).  Anyway somehow Nude didn’t work for me on the car. That’s when I discovered that I only listen to Nude when I’m conscious. When I’m in my surreal and subconscious and seeing through the world with artificial tears in my eyes (I want to say there are no tears, but I always like to think there are on my eyes in these moments. I’m the kind that cries a lot when I was a child but never afterwards), and the reflections of the light from the innocent but brutal world flow and dances through these tears, I need something even more visceral. I put on Sail to the Moon. And it fit the feeling perfectly. It combines that subconscious feeling above with the No Surprises experience. It’s my most out of world experience to date...until I woke up fully mind clear back online again when I arrived in school and writing this experience in retrospect. So, what do you think?

title: mitch mitchell in the who
content: I really felt Mitch Mitchell is a better drummer for the Who than Keith Moon due to his balance for technics and power and mood that’s needed to fully maximise the emotional impact of Townshend lyrics instead of crazy drumming.

title: most intellectually intense cinema rundown so far
content: Kurosawa's Dreams and Akira (animated film) makes me so emo and mind-bending. I was planning to watch Greta Garbo films to calm myself the fuck down but it requires me to devote energy (because the film is silent, and I'm not yet comfortable with watching silent films).
